description: No literal copying of a reference site's proprietary copy imagery or exact CSS values into the new build -- only the underlying decisions (palette family type pairing layout pattern) are reused
why_it_matters: Copying literal assets or copy is an IP risk and produces a worse, uncredited clone rather than a design that actually fits the new product

description: Colors are pulled from actual computed styles (preview_inspect, browser devtools, or scripts/ui-ux/scan.py's regex scan) rather than guessed by eye from a screenshot
why_it_matters: Eyeballed hex values from a screenshot are reliably wrong by enough to shift a whole palette's mood -- measurement is cheap and removes the guesswork

description: The reference's font pairing style weight contrast and letter-spacing tendency are named explicitly (e.g. 'high-contrast serif display + neutral grotesk body') rather than described as 'looks modern'
why_it_matters: Vague typographic descriptions don't survive translation into an actual type choice -- naming the specific pairing logic is what makes the brief actionable

description: The reference's layout is described with a specific named pattern (asymmetric hero, broken grid, full-bleed section, sticky nav) rather than a vague 'clean and modern' description
why_it_matters: A named pattern can be directly implemented; a vague aesthetic description can't -- this is the difference between a usable brief and a mood board

check: Motion cues noted or explicitly marked absent
description: If motion/interaction detail is visible via a live browser tool it's captured (e.g. 'restrained, only hover-state color shifts'); if only a static fetch was possible this is explicitly marked as unknown rather than silently omitted
why_it_matters: Silently omitting motion analysis reads later as if motion was deliberately ignored rather than genuinely unobservable from the available tooling

check: Multiple references reconciled to one direction
description: When 2-3 example sites are given, the brief identifies the shared thread they have in common and builds around that -- it does not stitch each site's most distinctive feature into one mismatched collage
why_it_matters: A collage of different sites' signature moves reads as incoherent and undermines the credibility of the reference-driven approach entirely

description: The brief states explicitly what's being changed from the reference and why, not just what's being borrowed
why_it_matters: Undocumented full-fidelity copying is indistinguishable from cloning; a stated divergence is what makes the output original design work informed by references rather than a copy

check: Reference checked at mobile viewport too
description: The reference site's mobile rendering is checked (via preview_resize or a mobile user-agent fetch), not just its desktop layout
why_it_matters: A brief built from desktop-only observation misses how the reference actually handles the layout collapse that the new build will also need to solve

description: The reference site's contrast ratios and text sizing are independently checked against this skill's a11y rules rather than assumed acceptable just because the reference site uses them
why_it_matters: A reference site being visually appealing says nothing about whether its contrast or type scale actually passes accessibility checks -- inheriting those flaws silently defeats the a11y module's purpose

check: Design brief confirmed with user before code generation
description: The Reference Design Brief is shared with the user (or client) and gets an explicit confirmation before any frontend code is generated from it
why_it_matters: A misread reference caught at the brief stage costs a sentence to fix; the same misread caught after a full page is built costs a rebuild
